## [00:00 - 03:06]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. I want to talk to you about an interesting technology that we have in many webinar platforms that allows us to cross-broadcast our webinar to Facebook or YouTube Live. So some of these services now allow you to basically have your webinar running just like you normally would, but then in addition to that, stream it live on Facebook or YouTube. So the question comes down to why in the world would you want to do this? Well, Facebook and YouTube doing it will bring additional attendees to your webinar, many of them who wouldn't come to your webinar otherwise. So this is because of the way that Facebook and YouTube have designed their live broadcast capabilities. They do things like automatically alerting your followers that you're live. And both of these services, because they want to promote people watching basically live TV on their service, actually give the video after you're done, gives it preferential treatment by showing it, exposing it to a higher number of followers than you would receive if you just put your video uploaded your video to their service. And so basically what ends up happening is you get the double effect of people being alerted to watch it live and more people getting exposed to it afterwards. So what ends up happening is that you get more viewers, including some who wouldn't have watched your webinar otherwise, because they just don't do webinars. And that'll happen both while you're live and afterwards. Now there are some disadvantages. Number one, they probably won't watch your entire webinar. And since your entire webinars, the sales offer comes at the end of that webinar, if they're dropping out before that offer happens, they never even see the offer. And they'll probably watch less of that webinar than they would if they were actually registered to watch that webinar. They tend to have a lower view time in a webinar, especially if that webinar is using slides like you're seeing here on this screen as opposed to face to camera. And you're probably going to have a lower percentage of buyers from these channels than you will from your webinar. So if 100 people watch your webinar, X percentages will buy. If 100 people watch you in a live, it'll be some number lower than X who will actually buy on a traditional basis. And part of that is just because a large percentage of them are watching on a cellular phone or something,

## [03:06 - 06:11]

so they're worried that their battery is going to run out, they haven't got time to sit there and watch it. So when they're like home sitting in front of a computer and turn that on, it can just go much longer, as well as all the buying elements seem to just work better on a desktop or a tablet than it does on their cell phone. One more disadvantage, you don't get the email address of those who watched on Facebook Live and YouTube Live. So you can't run the rest of the email funnel to them. But some argue that these disadvantages are actually outweighed by the greater reach that these live broadcasts provide. So what do you do? Well, one option I just want to throw out here is to broadcast only the first X minutes. 30 minutes is a pretty good number of your webinar. So you're only broadcasting say 30 minutes of your webinar on Facebook or YouTube Live. Then you're telling people that if they want to watch the rest, they need to click the link, either in the comments or you give them a URL to sign up and join the webinar. And you do this both verbally and in the comments. And so what this does is it basically starts them off in the Facebook and YouTube Live environment. And then you get higher conversion rate because you're moving them from that environment where they're distracted with everything going on around them in that environment to your environment where you control it because it's your webinar. And so this ends up improving your conversion rates. And it also reduces your reliance on these outside tools because one of the challenges is if it's Facebook or YouTube, it's technically their property. They can do whatever they want. And by the way, this is also important. Unless you are paying to advertise, you probably have terms of service challenges in both of those tools. And so both of those tools have at times. And they may have right now, depending on when you're listening to this, a prohibition against actually selling using their tool. And so if you have your content portion of your webinar happening on their tool, and then you move the sales piece onto your tool, your webinar, then you're no longer in violation of their terms of service. So just be aware of that because, hey, you can get hurt with this kind of thing. All right, so how do you technically do this? So you've got your normal webinar that you're going to do the way you normally do a webinar. Okay, so it's broadcast, people sign up for it, they receive a link, they go to a page and they watch the webinar there.

## [06:11 - 09:30]

So assuming that your webinar service has this capability and several of them do, your webinar service then actually has a connection that allows you to be able to click a button, do some connection stuff. And once you're done with that connection, then you're able to say, okay, I want to now go live onto Facebook or YouTube. And so the webinar service is connecting directly to Facebook live or to YouTube live. And so that's done within that service. And usually this is simple as clicking a button in the setup screen for that particular webinar that you'll then you'll verify and connect to your Facebook or your YouTube account. And by the way, you should actually do this long before the webinar starts because you don't need to be worrying about that stress just as you're coming up on the webinar. Then actually, as you're ready to start live broadcasting, you click another button that then pushes that in addition to streaming it out to your webinar platform, it also pushes it out to the Facebook or the YouTube platform. So check with your webinar provider if their technology allows you to do this. And if you also have the ability to literally turn it off part way through, which is an important part of this particular strategy. Now a couple of notes. It's against Facebook's developer terms of service for developers to create tools that broadcast live to multiple services at the same time. In other words, zoom has this capability of being able to push webinars out to Facebook live. But zoom as a developer is not allowed to also enable someone to broadcast live at the same time to YouTube live. That's against Facebook's developers terms of service. And so zoom wanted to keep their relationship with Facebook on the upper up doesn't allow you to broadcast to both places. That's why and I just use zoom as an example they're not the only ones who do this. But that's why most of them are going to force you to choose either one or the other. I recommend you choose the one where your audience is biggest and most engaged. Other notes, there will probably be about a 20 second or maybe even longer lag between your webinar and what's and when it actually appears on Facebook or YouTube. This allows your webinar service that software to reencode the video feed and push it out to YouTube or Facebook. Now you're going to need to take that into account for questions or polls and basically anytime you ask for feedback, it's going to be coming in 30 seconds, 20 seconds to I've had it actually I had one webinar where it was lagging by a minute and a half.

## [09:30 - 12:32]

And so I'm going along and I ask a question and the bulk of my audience was on Facebook live. I'd ask a question I get two responses. And then I sort of have to wait for a minute and a half and also I get this flood of other responses that are all coming from YouTube from Facebook live. And I got to tell you, it was a little distracting and a little bit confusing, especially the first couple of times when I realized what was happening. And I actually asked multiple questions. And I saw I was on like my second or third question. Also, I'm getting all these answers. And I don't know which one of those three questions they're answering. It was really confusing. And so I'm just bringing that up for you so that you recognize that that is the case. And so for warned helps you to do a better job of understanding what's really going on during your actually webinar. That also means and this is important. At least on zoom, I'll tell you that the first 20 seconds of live broadcast and what actually shows up on Facebook and YouTube will actually be the 20 seconds before you press the broadcast button. So let me explain it this way. So I've got a group of people they're showing up to my webinar, I'm doing the pre strategy and I'm having conversations with them and everything. And then I say, okay, we're now at the top of the hour, it's time to start the webinar. And so I go in and I press broadcast. What it does then is it at least on zoom will jump back 20 seconds before that point and start broadcasting from that point 20 seconds earlier. And so what ends up happening is someone who is live on Facebook and tuned into this, they start hearing this stuff that's totally out of context. They don't know what's going on. And it and that actually is what ends up being the recording. And so when people come to look at it afterwards, they press play and they see this stuff that they'd have no idea what's going on. And then 20 seconds later starts to resolve and hi, this is Don Crowley. Let's start this webinar. Well, in the meantime, they're gone. And so you need to recognize that at least with some services, zoom is a good example of this, that is going to happen. And so my solution is to actually start talking as if you're running your webinar for about 20 seconds and then press the button to start broadcasting live. Now this actually takes some practice. And so you're going to have to get used to it and you're going to have to rehearse it to figure out how all of this works. All right, now there is another option that you should be aware of.

## [12:32 - 15:39]

And that is you're running your normal webinar, but instead of relying on your webinar service to connect with Facebook or YouTube live, you can use an outside encoding tool to stream it. This can be hardware. So there are several hardware solutions out there that are quite fast and they're better quality.
